bibliographic_data.title: Survival analysis, the infinite Gaussian mixture model, FDG-PET and non-imaging data in the prediction of progression from mild cognitive impairment
bibliographic_data.abstract: We present a method to discover interesting brain regions in [18F] fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (PET) scans, showing also the benefits when PET scans are in combined use with non-imaging variables. The discriminative brain regions facilitate a better understanding of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) progression, and they can also be used for predicting conversion from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to AD. A survival analysis (Cox regression) and infinite Gaussian mixture model (IGMM) are introduced to identify the informative brain regions, which can be further used to make a prediction of conversion (in two years) from MCI to AD using only the baseline PET scan. Further, the predictive accuracy can be enhanced when non-imaging variables are used together with identified informative brain voxels. The results suggest that PET scan imaging data is more predictive than other non-imaging data, revealing even better performance when both imaging and non-imaging data are combined.

core_content.novelty_claim: The method combines survival analysis (Cox regression) and infinite Gaussian mixture model (IGMM) to identify informative brain regions from FDG-PET scans for predicting the conversion from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer’s disease (AD).
core_content.key_methodology: Survival analysis using Cox regression and infinite Gaussian mixture model (IGMM) are introduced to identify discriminative brain regions in FDG-PET scans. Non-imaging variables are also used to enhance predictive accuracy.
outcomes: Identified informative brain regions from FDG-PET scans
Enhanced prediction of conversion from MCI to AD using both imaging and non-imaging data
core_content.full_text_markdown:
# Introduction
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive, degenerative and incurable disease of the brain. The number of people suffering from dementia is expected to grow rapidly due to increasing life expectancy. Despite technological progress, ante mortem diagnosis of AD is still based on clinical grounds with biomarkers such as cerebrospinal fluid proteins and neuroimaging procedures providing supporting information.

Positron emission tomography with [18F] fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG-PET) has been widely applied to assist in the diagnosis of clinical AD. FDG-PET was recommended as a diagnostic marker by recently proposed guidelines. One important area where functional neuroimaging may add significant value is predicting the progression from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to full-blown AD dementia.

The main aim of this study is to apply survival analysis and the infinite Gaussian mixture model (IGMM) to FDG-PET scans in order to improve differentiation between patients who remain in MCI and those that progress to AD dementia, and to investigate the usefulness of non-imaging data compared to FDG-PET imaging data.

# Materials and Methods
Experiments were performed using data from the publicly available AD Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) database. Only data from the first stage of ADNI (ADNI 1) was considered, with a large pool of FDG-PET images acquired on various scanners using different imaging parameters. To eliminate bias due to these factors, images were selected that had been obtained using the same scanner and parameters such as the number of slices. Patients with a Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR) sum of the boxes score of 1.5, 2, or 2.5 were included, and patients were only considered if they had at least five consecutive clinical assessments every six months up to a maximum of 24 months.

The study population characteristics are summarized in Table 1.
